items.why: Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: How long does information typically last in short-term memory without rehearsal, according to Peterson and Peterson?
items.why: It's 18 seconds. “30 seconds” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Whose 1932 'War of the Ghosts' study demonstrated reconstructive memory?
items.why: It's Bartlett. “Atkinson” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Atkinson and Shiffrin propose the multi-store model?
items.why: The real figure is 1968 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
